main_summary.summary: C. T. Vivian, ordained minister and SCLC director of affiliates, recorded this oral history with Taylor Branch on March 29, 2011, at his home in Atlanta for the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Born in Howard County, Missouri, raised in Macomb, Illinois, after his grandmother's house was burned by an arsonist during the Depression, Vivian traces his civil rights work from a 1947 CORE-inspired sit-in at Peoria lunch counters through American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville, the Jim Lawson nonviolence workshops, and the April 19, 1960, march to Nashville City Hall after attorney Z. Alexander Looby's home was bombed. He describes riding the first reinforcement Freedom Ride bus into Jackson in May 1961, his beating at Parchman Farm, and SCLC campaigns in Birmingham, St. Augustine, and Selma, including his February 1965 confrontation with Sheriff Jim Clark at the Dallas County courthouse. Vivian's wife Octavia, Coretta Scott King's biographer, is woven through the narrative.
key_themes: Nonviolent direct action grounded in Christian theology
Nashville movement and the Jim Lawson workshops
Freedom Rides and Parchman Farm imprisonment
SCLC organizational strategy under Martin Luther King Jr.
Children's role in the Birmingham campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
Vivian provides first-person testimony from inside SCLC's organizational core during the Nashville sit-ins, the 1961 Freedom Rides reinforcement bus, the Birmingham Project C campaign, the 1964 St. Augustine movement, and the 1965 Selma voting-rights campaign. His role as SCLC's national director of affiliates places him at the operational seam between Martin Luther King Jr.'s strategic leadership, Wyatt Tee Walker's chief-of-staff function, and the local campaigns in Birmingham, Chattanooga, and Selma.

The interview preserves Vivian's framing of the movement as a moral and spiritual confrontation rather than primarily a legal one, his account of the Looby-bombing march in Nashville as a model that combined spontaneous mobilization with prior planning, his eyewitness narrative of the trustees at Hinds County Jail responding to the Freedom Riders' dignified conduct, and his theological reading of his Parchman beating and Selma courthouse encounter as religious experiences.

chapters.title: Missouri Roots and Illinois Move
chapters.summary: Vivian traces his birth in Howard County, Missouri, his family's loss of three farms during the Depression, and the arson that destroyed his grandmother's Boonville home after she sheltered a woman from a common-law husband. His mother and grandmother moved the family to Macomb, Illinois, in 1930 so he could attend a non-segregated school in a college town.

chapters.title: Peoria Sit-In and Meeting Octavia
chapters.summary: Vivian describes the 1947 CORE-inspired sit-in he and chemist Ben Alexander led at Peoria lunch counters, thirteen years before Greensboro, using techniques developed by Jim Farmer in Chicago. He meets his future wife Octavia at Carver Community Center in Peoria; they marry on February 22, 1954. Octavia's prior activism in Pontiac, Michigan, included voter registration with her physician's youth group.

chapters.title: Family Lineage and Naming
chapters.summary: Vivian recounts his great-great-grandfather's escape from slavery in Missouri to fight with the Union Army and his refusal to take his former owner's surname, taking his wife's Tyndall name instead. Octavia's family came from Arkansas rice country, descended from a great-great-great-grandfather brought from Africa for his knowledge of rice cultivation and then enslaved. Vivian discusses his unsuccessful search for other Vivians in Vivian, Louisiana, and elsewhere.

chapters.title: Call to Ministry, Move to Nashville
chapters.summary: Working at Helen Gallagher Foster's mail-order company in Peoria, Vivian receives an auditory call to ministry. His pastor has saved money for him to attend American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville rather than Colgate Rochester. Octavia, pregnant with their first son, presses him to honor the call. The seminary becomes the nexus where he meets Kelly Miller Smith, Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, and John Lewis, and where SCLC affiliates in Nashville begin to form.

chapters.title: Lawson Workshops in Nashville
chapters.summary: Vivian describes Jim Lawson's arrival in Nashville as Fellowship of Reconciliation field secretary, training students and ministers in nonviolent direct action after returning from India. He observes the workshops where cigarettes were extinguished on participants and eye contact was practiced. Diane Nash, Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, John Lewis, and white Vanderbilt students participated. Vivian distinguishes SCLC's eye-contact-grounded nonviolence from what he characterizes as SNCC's fetal-position posture.

chapters.title: The Looby March, April 1960
chapters.summary: After Z. Alexander Looby's home was bombed on April 19, 1960, Vivian and other Nashville leaders mobilized students from Tennessee A&I, Fisk, Meharry, and Pearl High School. Approximately 4,000 marchers proceeded silently into downtown Nashville, modeled on W.E.B. Du Bois's NAACP silent anti-lynching marches of the 1920s. At City Hall, Diane Nash's questioning of Mayor Ben West produced his public concession that segregation could not be justified; lunch counters opened within a week.
related_events: Bombing of Z. Alexander Looby's home

chapters.title: Freedom Rides into Jackson
chapters.summary: After the Anniston bus burning on Mother's Day 1961, Nashville movement leaders met at Robert Harris's home and decided not to let violence defeat nonviolence. Vivian joined the reinforcement bus from Montgomery to Jackson, Mississippi, with Colonel Birdsong and the Mississippi militia aboard. He describes patting the captain of police on the back at the Jackson bus terminal and asking to join the arrests. Inside the Hinds County jail the riders established a disciplined regimen of orderly meals, meditation periods, and strategy sessions.

chapters.title: Parchman Farm Beating
chapters.summary: Transferred from Jackson to Parchman Prison after roughly a week and a half, Vivian was interrogated by the warden, who asked whether he had syphilis. After Vivian answered no, guards beat him with a leather-encased lead slapjack until the edge cut his face and he bled. A guard then drew a .38 on him in the cell corridor but did not fire. The white freedom rider released early reported the beating to the press; Bobby Kennedy reportedly contacted Mississippi authorities within hours. Kelly Miller Smith drove to Parchman to take Vivian back to Nashville.

chapters.title: Chattanooga, SCLC Affiliates Director
chapters.summary: Vivian pastored in Chattanooga and led a voter-mobilization campaign that elected three-fifths of the city council across three elections, including a rainy election day. The strategy confronted the 20-percent paid Black vote with the message that voters could take the payment while voting their own preference. The Chattanooga results brought him to King's attention; Dorothy Cotton had also recommended him. Wyatt Tee Walker recruited him to Atlanta as SCLC's national director of affiliates, the parallel to NAACP branch coordination.

chapters.title: Birmingham Project C
chapters.summary: Vivian's first SCLC assignment was Birmingham. He and Wyatt Tee Walker opened the campaign office, detecting federal surveillance when men on a nearby telephone-company balcony retreated indoors during each call. On May 3, 1963, as fire hoses turned on demonstrators at the park, Vivian and Walker routed marchers out the church's back door to buses bound for downtown, occupying the business district while Bull Connor's hoses were still trained on the diversion. The dogs and hoses imagery brought ministers from across the country into the campaign.

chapters.title: St. Augustine 1964
chapters.summary: Vivian frames the 1964 St. Augustine campaign as instrumental to keeping the civil-rights bill moving through Congress during the filibuster. He describes a beach wade-in where local white swimmers formed a human net to push him toward deeper water until Fred Shuttlesworth joined him and a policeman pulled an attacker off after Vivian was forced underwater. He details midnight marches past the old slave market, Hoss Manucy's Klan attacks, and the night about 40 marchers were sent to the hospital after objects were thrown from walls.

chapters.title: Selma and Sheriff Jim Clark
chapters.summary: As SCLC's national director of affiliates, Vivian scouted Selma for the voting-rights campaign and recognized that the action encompassed not just the city but the surrounding Black Belt counties. On February 15, 1965, he confronted Sheriff Jim Clark at the Dallas County courthouse steps with the named-badge dialogue that he describes as having arrived to him unscripted. The beating that followed sent him to Good Samaritan Hospital and then to a Selma jail, where he describes another moment in an elevator when a deputy struck him with a billy club that he partly cushioned with his hands.

chapters.title: Reflections on King and Legacy
chapters.summary: Vivian discusses Ralph Abernathy's decision to defer leadership to King despite Abernathy's own larger Montgomery church and stronger Alabama roots, King's facility with older staff egos, and King's framing of change as moral and spiritual rather than legal. He recounts King's race-horse joke and his understanding of timing. Vivian closes on his post-movement project to train one million Black students per year to pass the ACT at high levels, arguing that freedom must be followed by education. A final story recounts Charles Billups leading the Birmingham Sunday Red Sea March where firemen refused Bull Connor's order to turn on the hoses.
